sentences.text: There already, 60.
We were living with the peasants.
You see, we were working, and we paid them, or worked for them.
You see, worked in the call halls-- we gave them our coats.
What we had, what we could give, we gave to them.
And they let us live as long as we could give them, you see.
As I told you, we were a productive element.
We had some tailors between us.
We had some who become improvise hair dressers, the shaving and these different.
My father was specialist in tobacco.
And he showed them how to dry the tobacco and this.
And I myself worked in the stables.
And we all were appreciated by the local population because we worked--
--and helped them.
We helped each other as well, you see.
And we tried to help survive.
Unfortunately, many died in the first winter.
It was typhoid, hunger-- less hunger, more cold.
And that went on until the spring, which came late in this year.
But in-- it was 16th of June, I was working in the forest, with a group in the forest, trying to get contact with the partisan.
It was a local partisan group.
And I tried to get contact with the partisan.
I was working with a old Ukrainian peasant of-- he took with him in the forest for the winter to prepare-- food.
And we came back.
On the way back, I saw a carriage with Romanian gendarme with my father in the middle.
They were after me.
So of course, I changed places, and they took me away.
They took me in the school as detention.
My friend the local priest came with bread and vodka.
And the chief of police, of militia there, told me he will close his eye if I run away-- to run away because they take all the young Jews, work able, labor [INAUDIBLE] away.
But I couldn't.
My parents were there, my sister.
So I had to stay put.
We were taken all to Shargorod.
From Shargorod, we were taken along the highway to-- back to-- to Mogilev, where we were brought to the station.
There were already German standing around.
And over the entrance they had arbeit macht frei.
They had the ghetto.
They were enclosed in the ghetto where you had to pass through the ghetto.
I saw some friends, but went through.
There were already from other places Jews, a whole train standing.
And they told us they had taken, the previous day, one transport for Mogilev.
It goes to the coal mines, the peat exploitation near [NON-ENGLISH], Nestervarka.
So I went up to [NON-ENGLISH].
From there we were escorted by a Romanian and Germans because in [NON-ENGLISH] were a German garrison and the Gestapo.
They had a headquarters there.
To Nestervarka and put in the stables of the local call house, which was empty.
And there were about a thousand of us.
[INAUDIBLE] from different place.
I found a cousin of mine.
All we heard what happened in other places.
Some escaped death, particularly in [NON-ENGLISH].
Next [? to ?] no food, only a pill.
That means beating, beating, beating.
Their dogs, which were rounding us up.
Next morning, off to work.
We were driven by escort [INAUDIBLE] a Romanian and Ukrainian militia up to the workplace.
The had an exploitation of peat there.
They put us to dig peat.
They had pumps there where they worked.
First, they had the-- a group of Lipovan.
They were a sect from Bessarabia-- from Bessarabia which were specialized in [INAUDIBLE] work.
They had there [INAUDIBLE] took these carriages [COUGHS], pardon, the peat away.
And there were beatings.
They had there Romanian veteran war, I couldn't [INAUDIBLE] them, the invalid's, which were from the front, which were mostly Iron Guards.
The Jews-- [INAUDIBLE] they had pieces of rubber pipe, some filled with iron-- they were beating.
I had to work forwards, forwards, and German came only in inspection.
The head of the camp was Romanian Hungarian.
He was from Transylvania, Lekatosz.
He was a beast.
He enjoyed beating.
And then, we had particular I remember, it was a Romanian officer, which-- which lost an eye on the front, which hated Jewish women.
And he was beating them.
I will tell you afterwards why I remember him so well.
I worked there first in digging.
We were given, in the morning, a bit of hot water.
Noon time, they recorded pea broth.
I heard afterwards-- I didn't realize at the time-- they were poisoned peas, peas and beans we had.
And a piece pea polenta-- from peas made.
They were really trying to see how long you could survive with this food.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I worked from June up to end of September in the turf-- a peat exploitation.
Then one of the Romanian supervisor was a student in mining engineering, needed tutoring in mathematics.
I don't know how he got to know that I am mathematician.
So he asked me if I want-- he wants tutoring.
Today he took me off two hours or three hours to tutor him in mathematics.
What I got out from this was three hours less work under these inhuman conditions.
He left on the table bread, a piece of cheese, which I swallowed, of course.
He closed his eyes.
He couldn't give me, so-- and I collected rest of cigarettes, which were on the table.
I collected, brought them with me, which was for barter very good.
I-- most important, he was reading papers, the German army papers, and the Romanian papers.
So I could read from all sorts of papers.
I don't know if he saw it or not, because we were not permitted to read papers.
And I was expected when I came in the-- I became the newspaper of the camp.
I brought always-- sometime I adjusted it in our form to give them hope.
In particular, [INAUDIBLE] retreat in some places.
So was a big retreat.
But it helped the people to keep up the morale.
And he wanted me near, so he put me as sort of an [INAUDIBLE] clerk in the office.
I had to check how many people at work are there in different parts of the mine.
And to measure the ready production, which was sent to a former synagogue, which had been transformed, or was being-- was transformed, also by Jewish forced labors, into a factory to make bricks.
Jews also worked on the railway from the synagogue to the railway station.
It was not a pleasant work, always beating, beating, beating.
We survived, and in-- it was in September.
It was at the time of Yom Kippur, I remember.
I had heard that he-- what he did with the Jewish women.
He used to try to the breasts, and the Jewish woman push back her chest.
So hit in the genitalia, or hit in genitalia and then to grab the breast.
I have heard this and I said, I will not be able to resist hitting him.
I don't-- could not remember.
I cannot remember.
He was a invalid from the front.
Officer, but invalid-- he lost an eye that had black [INAUDIBLE] on.
One day it happened that I hit him.
Thanks to my pupil, if I could call it so, I was not executed this.
But it was in beginning of November, it was cold.
They came one day, and they ask for work.
For-- it was supposed to be for an aerodrome that the Germans were building.
So one group was sent to the aerodrome, and the next day another group.
They had the list.
They called the names out this, this, this.
We had numbers after-- and the next day, I was one of these on the list.
He arranged for me to be on the list.
My pupil was not in the camp at the time.
So I went.
We were sent from one place to the other.
We ended up in [NON-ENGLISH].
That was a stone exploitation, which I don't know if they use for railway.
I don't know what they use it.
There already the Jews working there.
And they were German there supervising.
We came there in the evening.
Of course, beatings.
They put us together in a corner there.
Towards the morning, two trucks appeared, and they took Jews.
And a group of Ukrainian were there.
They're a mixed group.
There were also some political I heard there, which were not Jews.
On them, and took us away.
We were brought to the Bug, and which we had to cross.
It was a foot bridge really.
It was not for carriages.
And then they started on, on, on-- were men and women.
And in my group, I couldn't find for my old friends.
We came, and the whole day we were driven without food, water-- nothing.
And we came to a place where we saw they had machine guns there.
And German soldier were there, officers.
And Ukrainian militia was there.
They told us-- it was bitter cold-- but still they told us to undress.
And shoes-- I had a pair of Dutch, with wooden, and brown uniform, the camp uniform.
My clothes were gone I had, that they gave us.
And to put it nice in order there in place, and to go.
It was what I think is a anti-tank pit.
They put us in front, and the machine guns, and some soldiers with guns, rifles.
And when they commanded [NON-ENGLISH], I understood what it is.
I saw behind me there were people dead already laying there.
I didn't see them when they killed them, but they were waiting for us.
And when I heard this, I decided I throw myself in.
I didn't hear fire.
I didn't hear.
When they started, I threw myself in.
I didn't have a scratch.
I swear.
But I don't know what happened afterwards.
Three days in my life-- complete amnesia.
I was found by some Ukrainian peasant from a village [NON-ENGLISH].
This I heard afterwards.
They found me running.
It was a forest there.
I was running in a trauma, speaking Romanian, and German, and French, and every language.
There was all in black covered.
I don't know.
Was it-- they told me.
I don't remember anything about it.
And they couldn't make out who I am.
So they took me to the village.
It was a mayor there.
I was the-- the connection with the local partisan group.
It was a group led by Storozheshin, a member of the-- high member of the party, Communist Party, which was part of the famous Korpak partisan unit.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
They brought me there.
They interrogated me.
I told them the truth, I am a Jewish deportee.
And how I escaped, I don't know.
The last what I knew, was that I let myself drop backwards in the pit.
He asked me question different, what I was and how-- what I did under the Russian when they were in Bucovina and so on.
I spoke with him in his lingo.
He saw I bona fide And [INAUDIBLE] he told me, would you like to join?
I was in a very bad state, hungry.
And they-- they gave me to drink.
They gave me-- it was a particular paramedic, if it were, feldsher, paramedic.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Aleksandar Vasilyevich Tizhnivoi was his name.
He saved me really.
He bandage me, and he saved me really, without cold compresses.
And he brought me to life.
And he asked me, do you want to help me?
Do you know first aid and this because we need it?
So I worked first with him.
When I came to myself I could already walk and run.
So they [INAUDIBLE], oh, you will help us.
You have to join.
We moved all the time.
We were near a place, Berdichev.
And there was this large German garrison.
And they asked me to go in the village, dress as Ukrainian youngster, and to offer my services to the kitchen, a German kitchen.
So I went in.
I didn't speak to show that I understand German this [INAUDIBLE].
I [GERMAN] and made the [INAUDIBLE] clean.
So they took me in the kitchens, were only too glad, for there was two soldiers working.
So they were only glad to give me the job.
I understood everything.
There were German and Walon there.
They were volunteers from Belgium was there, and a Slovak unit, which were in-- in Berdichev.
Afterwards, I heard they had also labor soldier, Hungarian, but I didn't see them.
I worked with them a few days, and then I had my-- all the time was in-- in connection-- I had my connection to my unit.
And when they were ready to go, our transport, they had very small garrisons in villages.
We sent them ammunition and food out.
So I-- I know it's myself and-- and my group.
They giving a reception.
I had to run back to my group because they would have known that I am the one who was a traitor, if I could call it so.
Then we were-- we went all the time in Vinnytsia Oblast.
From there, we went into Galicia, always attacking German garrison, putting-- mining the railways to killing.
And we went up to the Carpathian as we knew we were supposed to go over the Carpathian in Slovakia where they prepared in Banska Bystrica a resistance out of all the Slovaks.
But we never reached it.
We were-- the local population, [NON-ENGLISH], gave us over to the German, OR sold for sugar.
And nearly all our group was killed.
We returned 27 on three sledges back to Ukraine-- Vinnytsia, from where our unit came.
There we started again getting orders from Korpak, our commanding officer.
It was in the time of Stalingrad, Korsun-Shevchenkivskyi where the big battles were.
Our job was to cut the supply lines of the Germans, to kill as many Germans, destroy the railway lines.
That was our job.
We-- that happened in the forest near [NON-ENGLISH], a plane landed and brought the political commissar of the partisan movement in the Ukraine, the renowned leader Nikita Khrushchev to us.
I got a medal from him for distinction in my work in the partisan movement.
Other communists got even higher order, and officers rank It happened we fought all around in the region.
Korsun-Shevchenkivskyi was a bloodbath.
All the German which were cut off, and the Russian army came nearer.
And we were met-- met the Red Army units.
And we went with them to Vinnytsia-- liberated Vinnytsia.
That was a main town of the region.
From there, we got the order to go to the east, towards Nyester, to cut off the German troops which were fighting there.
It was von Manstein, I remember, the army group-- that was the commanding officer, General Manstein.
We--
That was in '43.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we, because we were going on my home town, so I wanted to be with the unit which was-- I knew the region.
I was with-- together with the unit.
In Vinnytsia, I met the first-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let us call officer from the field bank.
And I told them, I am also a trained bank clerk.
And they told me-- gave me a paper-- when you come, when you are liberated, your town, you will be organizing the bank-- banking system in the newly liberated territory.
It took, in March-- 23rd of March, if-- if I remember well, we liberated my home town.
I was with the first troop-- I entered the home town.
I was sort of guiding them.
I was still in partisan side, had my own weapon.
Entered the town-- it took two or three days still, the town was cleaned of the German.
I found my great grandfather there still.
He was not deported.
A sister and my sister, my late father and the uncle in the town.
They were not deported.
And I went to-- to become the organizer to reorganize the state bank.
I didn't have even shoes properly.
So I had to-- as a matter of fact, I had to buy a pair of shoes, which were a number smaller than I had.
I could under-- take off my shoes under the table as a big functionar, commissar-- because there were too small for me.
And so I had to appear.
I worked from beginning of April in this job, first in [NON-ENGLISH] where I worked before the war, afterwards in Strozhnitz.
And on the-- again, on the 16th of June was [INAUDIBLE] my day when I surprise, as it was the birthday of my late mother.
I was called up.
Probably they couldn't finish Hitler off without me.
I was called up in the Red Army.
From Strozhnitz, which is about 20 miles from Cerniauti, I came to Cerniauti.
There was replacement battalion, which recruited the soldier for-- called up the soldier and directed them to units.
They found out that I can read and write.
And they needed somewhat for clerical work in particular.
I had already the medal, and I was still in partisan uniform.
I had my own sub-machine gun.
And they took me as a clerk for the unit until July.
It was the beginning of July when I was sent to Kiev.
There were these two officers, which were in charge of this unit.
I've been to Kiev.
There it was a camp where they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] assembled units for the front.
It was not to very good situation.
You made exercise all the day on the tummy.
We had to run after food.
And the sergeants stole all the food and sold them to local population, which were hungry.
And so, I will not be able to go on like this.
So in the morning in the first appeal, I asked to speak to the commanding officer.
The adjutant who took our morning appeal asked me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to appear that morning in the front of the command-- commanding officer.
He asked me what nationality I am.
I told him Jewish.
He was very surprised that I want to go to the front.
He told me, next morning I see you.
Next morning, I appeared in front of the commanding officer.
He had same question, your name?
Nationality?
I told him Jew.
What do you want?
I want to the front.
He-- he, ironically, asked me if I want to the second front in the opposite direction.
And I told him not.
Against the German, I want to fight against the German.
So he said, OK, gave me a paper to go to take from the storing uniform, because I was still in my half civil, half German army uniform with the red sign on the head.
I took the new uniform, which I promptly sold in order to send some money-- because my parents in the meantime returned from deportation.
By the way, my father was born in this town where I was organizing.
And the evening, I was back in the unit.
They put me in a truck to the front.
The whole night we were in a column of trucks.
We arrived [NON-ENGLISH], if I remember, and there we stopped.
Yes.
